comments:
This paper addresses a practical and challenging problem: enabling reliable limited-vocabulary ASR for a single-microphone portable music playback device where the microphone sits very close to loudspeakers and speech-to-music ratios can be extremely low. The proposed front-end combines established robust acoustic echo cancellation, a coherence-based residual echo power estimator driven by a double-talk probability, an MMSE (LSA) noise suppressor, and a novel quasi-binary masking strategy that switches between LSA gains and binary masking based on estimated a priori SNR. In addition, the authors tune a large set of front-end parameters by directly optimizing ASR recognition rate using a genetic algorithm, and they train acoustic models on front-end processed noisy speech. Experimental validation includes both simulated noisy TIMIT data and a small real-world recording study with eight subjects and different playback levels; comparisons are reported against a POLQA-tuned variant and a no-enhancement baseline.

Strengths:
- The work targets a realistic, difficult deployment scenario (single mic near speakers, SERs down to −35 dB) of clear practical importance for consumer devices.
- The overall system design is technically sound and integrates multiple robust techniques; the quasi-binary masking idea is sensible for combining the robustness of MMSE at very low SNRs with the ASR benefits of masking at higher SNR bins.
- The task-driven tuning (optimizing ASR accuracy directly) is an appropriate and effective choice for this application and is shown to outperform POLQA-based perceptual tuning for recognition.
- Authors evaluate both on simulated corpora and real recordings, which strengthens the paper’s claims of applicability.

Weaknesses and suggestions for improvement:
- Quantitative reporting in the provided text is limited: the review references tables but the actual numerical results for the limited-vocabulary tests and per-condition recognition rates are not shown here. The paper should present complete numeric results, confidence intervals or significance testing, and confusion matrices for the limited-vocabulary task to better assess performance and failure modes.
- The real-world evaluation uses only eight subjects and a very small command set. More subjects, varied acoustic environments, and a larger test set would improve confidence in generalization.
- The genetic algorithm configuration appears small (three generations, population 40); more discussion on convergence, risk of overfitting to training conditions, computational cost of tuning, and how sensitive results are to initialization would be useful. An ablation study showing the contribution of individual components (e.g., cascaded RAECs, the DTP-based residual estimator, quasi-binary mask) to final performance would clarify which elements drive gains.
- Comparisons to other single-microphone enhancement approaches targeted at ASR (beyond POLQA tuning) are limited. A head-to-head comparison against representative recent single-mic enhancement/feature masking methods would strengthen the paper.
- Practical deployment concerns (real-time complexity, latency, CPU/memory requirements on the target device) are not discussed; these are important for consumer device applications.
- Reproducibility: provide parameter values found by GA, training recipes, and preferably code or a configuration file so others can reproduce results.

Overall, the paper presents a solid and practically relevant system with promising results. With more complete quantitative reporting, stronger real-world evaluations, ablation experiments, and more comparisons to related single-mic methods, the contribution would be considerably strengthened.
